 A slide-sized timeline of the key milestones in this lecture. The sequence matters: atrocity produces a code, ordinary abuses expose deeper problems, and oversight emerges only after repeated failure.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
8.1 What IRBs Do

IRBs review proposed human-subject research
Ask whether risks are minimized
Ask whether risks are justified
Ask whether subjects are selected fairly
Ask whether consent protections are adequate

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
8.2 How an IRB Thinks







irb


A

Protocol
submitted



B

 An IRB turns Belmont-style principles into review questions. Each gate reflects a lesson learned from earlier research abuses.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
8.3 Clinical Ethics Committees

IRBs govern research
Clinical ethics committees help with bedside conflict
They advise on capacity, surrogates, end-of-life decisions, and conflict
They are multidisciplinary by design
Their existence reflects distrust of one-person moral authority

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
section: 8.3 Why This Still Matters at the Bedside
text:
8.3 Why This Still Matters at the Bedside

The reforms that began in research ethics reshaped ordinary clinical ethics too.
Patients came to be seen as agents who decide, not passive recipients who are managed.
Consent, the right to refuse, truth-telling, and confidentiality moved from the margins to the center of care.
The governing question changed from “What should the doctor do?” to “How should clinicians decide with patients?”
That question is exactly where Part B begins.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
8.4 The Rulebook You Just Built

Together, these are why research today requires documented consent and independent review.







Scandal
What was missing
What it became




Carrie Buck (1927)
Your body is yours
The body-autonomy idea the law had skipped


Nuremberg (1947)
The subject’s free “yes”
Voluntary consent


Willowbrook & Beecher (1956–66)
An outside check
Independent review (IRBs)


Tuskegee (1932–72)
Honesty, treatment, fair selection
Belmont: respect, beneficence, justice

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
8.5 Wrapping Up Part A

Medical ethics moved from professional paternalism toward explicit respect for persons because abuse made the older model impossible to defend. Nuremberg named consent. Willowbrook and Beecher showed abuse can look respectable. Tuskegee exposed racism, deception, and 40 years of institutional failure. Belmont turned these lessons into a framework that still governs research and echoes through bedside care.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
8.6 References


Beecher, Henry K. 1966. “Ethics and Clinical Research.” New England Journal of Medicine 274 (24): 1354–60. https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJM196606162742405.


Jonsen, Albert R. 1998. The Birth of Bioethics. Oxford University Press.


National Commission for the Protection of Human Subjects of Biomedical and Behavioral Research. 1979. The Belmont Report: Ethical Principles and Guidelines for the Protection of Human Subjects of Research. U.S. Department of Health, Education, and Welfare. https://www.hhs.gov/ohrp/regulations-and-policy/belmont-report/index.html.


Nuremberg Military Tribunals. 1947. The Nuremberg Code. Trials of War Criminals before the Nuremberg Military Tribunals under Control Council Law No. 10.


Potter, Van Rensselaer. 1970. “Bioethics, the Science of Survival.” Perspectives in Biology and Medicine 14 (1): 127–53.


Supreme Court of the United States. 1927. Buck v. Bell, 274 U.S. 200 (1927). U.S. Supreme Court decision.


U.S. National Library of Medicine. 2002. Greek Medicine: The Hippocratic Oath. History of Medicine Division. https://www.nlm.nih.gov/hmd/greek/greek_oath.html.


World Medical Association. 1964. “Declaration of Helsinki: Ethical Principles for Medical Research Involving Human Subjects.” JAMA.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: A Trial Offer at the Worst Time
text:
A Trial Offer at the Worst Time

You have metastatic cancer. Your oncologist says:

“The standard drug has stopped working.”
“There is a study you could join, but I need to be clear: it is a randomized trial.”
“One group gets the current standard treatment. The other gets the new drug.”
“I do not get to choose your group, and neither do you.”
“I hope it helps you, but the study is designed to answer a question, not to tailor a plan just for you.”


Does this sound like care, research, or both?
What would you still want to know before saying yes?
If your doctor is the one recruiting you, what confusion is most likely?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


Three starting points matter here:

Research can happen inside ordinary care settings without becoming ordinary care. Same clinic, different goal.
Randomization is the clue. Once assignment is by chance, the clinician is no longer choosing only on the basis of what seems best for this patient.
Hope is not the same as a promise. Ethical recruitment does not kill hope, but it cannot quietly market the study as personalized treatment.





The opening move should make students feel how easily research can sound like an ordinary treatment recommendation. The point is not to make them anti-research; it is to make them notice the difference in purpose.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Today

By the end of Part A, you will be able to:

Distinguish clinical care from clinical research, and say why the difference changes consent and oversight
Explain randomization, control, placebo, and blinding by walking through the 1954 polio trial
Tell a clinical endpoint from a surrogate, and judge whether a result could be chance
Summarize what trial phases I–IV are for
Explain the therapeutic misconception and why it matters at the bedside
Use the opioid-evidence story to say what a given study can and cannot support

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
One Crucial Difference

Clinical care aims to help the patient in front of you
Clinical research aims to produce generalizable knowledge (Emanuel et al. 2000)
Both may benefit a patient
But direct patient benefit is not what makes an activity research
That difference changes consent, oversight, and acceptable risk

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: The Same Room Can Hide the Difference
text:
The Same Room Can Hide the Difference


Why it feels like care

Same hospital
Same white coats
Same lab draws and imaging
Same reassuring language of “options”
Often the same physician


Why it is not the same

The protocol fixes what can happen
Randomization may assign the arm
The main goal is learning, not tailoring
The results are meant to guide future care
Independent review is required before it begins

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Why Research Exists

Without research, medicine would freeze in place
New drugs, devices, and procedures need to be tested before they become standard
Even old treatments need comparison, follow-up, and correction
Ethical research is not the enemy of patient care
Ethical research is one reason patient care can improve at all

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: Research vs. Clinical Care at a Glance
text:
Research vs. Clinical Care at a Glance








Question
Clinical care
Clinical research




Main goal
Help this patient now
Produce reliable knowledge


Who should benefit first?
The individual patient
Future patients, and sometimes current participants


Who sets the plan?
Clinician and patient
Protocol, eligibility rules, and study design


What governs it?
Professional standards, licensure, ordinary consent
IRB review, protocol rules, research consent (U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, Office for Human Research Protections 2026)


What counts as success?
Good care for this person
Valid evidence that answers the research question

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
The Physician-Investigator Tension

A clinician may also be a physician-investigator
That can be efficient and even helpful
It can also blur whether advice is being given as a doctor or as a researcher
Modern research ethics assumes this tension is real, not rare (World Medical Association 2024)
That is one reason independent review matters

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Why Randomize?

If doctors chose the groups, healthier and sicker patients could cluster without anyone meaning to (National Cancer Institute 2024)
Randomization assigns people by chance
That helps keep the comparison scientifically fair
But it also means the clinician is no longer hand-picking the treatment for this patient
So randomization is ethically important, not just statistically useful

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: When Is It OK to Randomize a Sick Person?
text:
When Is It OK to Randomize a Sick Person?

The honest answer needs a concept: clinical equipoise
It means the expert community is genuinely unsure which arm is better
If we already knew, randomizing would be wrong — we’d be assigning some patients to the worse option on purpose
Equipoise is the moral license for a trial
This is why “I’d just give everyone the new drug” misses the point: we do not yet know it is better

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: One Map of a Parallel-Group Trial
text:
One Map of a Parallel-Group Trial







rct


A

People with the
condition



B

Eligibility check
and consent



A->B





C

Randomize



B->C





D

Arm 1
new treatment



C->D





E

Arm 2
standard care or
control



C->E





F

Follow-up
and outcome data



D->F





E->F





G

Compare results



F->G






 A plain-language map of a parallel-group randomized trial. The key ethical moment is the middle: once participants consent and are randomized, no one chooses the arm just for that patient’s benefit.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
What the Control Group Gets

Sometimes the control group gets current standard care
Sometimes it gets standard care plus a placebo
Sometimes, if no proven treatment exists, it may get placebo or no active treatment (National Cancer Institute 2024; World Medical Association 2024)
Control does not mean “ignored”
Everyone in the study is still monitored under a protocol

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Placebo Does Not Always Mean “Nothing”

A placebo can do different jobs.

If no proven treatment exists, placebo may stand in for no active treatment.
If a proven treatment already exists, placebo is often added on top of standard care so no one is denied basic treatment.
The ethical question is not just “placebo or no placebo?” It is what participants are missing by being in that arm (National Cancer Institute 2024; World Medical Association 2024).

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
What Blinding Actually Does

Blinding means some people in the trial do not know which arm a participant is in
Single-blind usually means the participant does not know
Double-blind usually means the participant and the treating team or evaluators do not know
The point is to reduce expectation effects and biased judgment
“Double-blind” is about cleaner evidence, not secrecy for its own sake

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
A Tiny Example

If patients know they are receiving the new drug, they may report feeling better partly because they expect it to work. If clinicians know who got the new drug, they may judge borderline improvements more generously. Blinding tries to reduce that problem.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: Who Watches the Trial While It Runs?
text:
Who Watches the Trial While It Runs?

A trial is not left alone until it ends
An independent data safety monitoring board (DSMB) reviews the data as it accumulates
It can stop a trial early for harm — one arm is clearly hurting people
It can also stop early for benefit — one arm is so clearly better that continuing the other is no longer fair
Equipoise has a deadline: once the evidence is in, the trial must end

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Four Phases, Four Questions








Phase
The main question
Typical scale




I
Is it safe? What dose can people tolerate?
Small


II
Does it seem to work?
Larger


III
Is it better than the current standard?
Large, often randomized


IV
What happens after approval in the real world?
Very large, longer-term

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
One Simple Phase Map







phases


A

Phase I
small group
safety and dose



B

Phase II
more participants
early evidence of effect



A->B





C

Phase III
large comparison
against standard care



B->C





D

Phase IV
after approval
long-term follow-up



C->D






 A plain-language phase map. The main ethical point is that ‘clinical trial’ covers activities with very different goals, risks, and likely benefits.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Phase I: Start with Safety

In cancer research, Phase I often involves about 15 to 30 people (National Cancer Institute 2024)
Researchers ask what dose is tolerable and what harms appear
They also look for signs the intervention is doing anything biologically
But direct benefit is not the main point
This is why Phase I can be ethically difficult: risk may be significant while personal upside is limited

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: Phase II: Does It Look Promising?
text:
Phase II: Does It Look Promising?

More people are enrolled than in Phase I
The question shifts toward whether the treatment seems to work
Safety is still being watched closely
A positive Phase II result is encouraging, not conclusive
Many interventions fail here or later

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: Phase III: Against the Current Standard
text:
Phase III: Against the Current Standard

Phase III asks whether a new treatment outperforms the best existing option or changes the balance of harms and benefits
Randomization is common here (National Cancer Institute 2024)
Sample sizes are much larger
This is where placebo and control-group debates become most visible
The outcome matters not just for one patient but for what becomes the new standard

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Phase IV: After Approval

Approval is not the end of learning
Phase IV tracks long-term safety and effectiveness in broader populations (National Cancer Institute 2024)
Rare side effects may appear only after many people use a drug
Real-world use is messier than tightly controlled trials
So ethics does not stop once a treatment reaches the market

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Why Phases Matter Ethically

Students often hear “clinical trial” and imagine one thing
But Phase I and Phase IV do not ask the same question or offer the same likely benefits
Consent should match the phase, not rely on a generic script
Risks, hopes, and expectations shift from phase to phase
So the ethical explanation has to be specific

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
The Therapeutic Misconception

Therapeutic misconception happens when participants think the study intervention is being chosen mainly for their personal medical benefit (Appelbaum et al. 1982)
The misunderstanding is not just “I hope this helps”
It is more like “my doctor is selecting this because it is best for me”
That is often false once protocol rules and randomization control the options
A person can sign a form and still misunderstand the basic situation

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Why It Happens So Easily

Recruitment often comes from a trusted clinician
Serious illness makes hope urgent
Research visits look like treatment visits
Words like “option” and “opportunity” blur categories
Many people hear “new” and assume “better”

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: A Research Consent Is Not a Treatment Consent
text:
A Research Consent Is Not a Treatment Consent

Valid consent has four elements; research adds requirements on top of them
It must say, in plain words, “this is research” — not treatment chosen for you
It must disclose randomization: a chance process, not your clinician, may pick your arm
It must state the goal is knowledge, and that direct benefit is uncertain or unlikely
It must make withdrawal at any time, without penalty, explicit
The nurse is often the one who notices a form was signed but not understood

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
The Dangerous Sentence


“This study may help you” can be true. “This study is being chosen because it is the best treatment for you” is often false.


The distinction is what the therapeutic misconception tries to protect (Appelbaum et al. 1982; Emanuel et al. 2000).

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: Early-Phase Trials and Thin Personal Benefit
text:
Early-Phase Trials and Thin Personal Benefit

Healthy volunteers and desperate patients join studies for very different reasons
In Phase I, researchers mainly ask what dose is tolerable and what harms appear
Some participants may benefit, but many will not
Payment, hope, and lack of alternatives can all cloud judgment
Ethical recruitment has to say this plainly

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Thought Question

A new drug’s Phase III trial is reported as “positive.”

It significantly improved a surrogate marker on a scan.
The trial was small, and about 25% of participants dropped out.
The analysis included only those who completed.


Name the three weaknesses you can already see.
What would you want to know before telling a patient this drug “works”?
Is “the FDA might approve it” the same as “it helps patients”?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Make It Contemporary

Regulators sometimes grant accelerated approval based on a surrogate endpoint, with a confirmatory trial promised later.

Patients with no other option get the drug years sooner.
Some confirmatory trials later show no real benefit — and a few are never finished.

Is faster access worth weaker evidence? Who should decide, and what should happen if the confirmatory trial fails?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


Three distinctions to carry forward:

Statistically significant ≠ clinically meaningful. A real but tiny effect on a surrogate can be true and still not worth having.
Regulatory approval ≠ proven patient benefit. Approval is a decision under uncertainty, not a guarantee.
The standing questions are always the same: what was measured, in whom, and who is missing from the result?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
The Main Distinction

Trials are ethically hard because they borrow the setting of care but not its single-minded goal
Randomization, blinding, control groups, and phases are scientific tools
The same tools also let you read a result: what was measured, could it be chance, who is missing
Those tools are exactly why research needs its own ethics
Part B turns from the structure of trials to the rulebook meant to protect the people inside them

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Wrapping Up Part A

Clinical research is not ordinary care with extra paperwork. Its purpose is to produce reliable knowledge, which is why it uses randomization, control groups, blinding, and staged phases — visible in one real instrument, the 1954 polio trial, and again in the COVID-19 vaccine trials. Those same tools also teach you to read a study: clinical versus surrogate endpoints, whether a result could be chance, and who dropped out or was left out. The key task in Part A is learning to say, clearly and without panic: this is research, not just treatment by another name — and here is how good its evidence actually is.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
References


Appelbaum, Paul S., Loren H. Roth, and Charles Lidz. 1982. “The Therapeutic Misconception: Informed Consent in Psychiatric Research.” International Journal of Law and Psychiatry 5 (3-4): 319–29. https://doi.org/10.1016/0160-2527(82)90026-7.


Emanuel, Ezekiel J., David Wendler, and Christine Grady. 2000. “What Makes Clinical Research Ethical?” JAMA 283 (20): 2701–11. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.283.20.2701.


Francis, Thomas et al. 1955. “An Evaluation of the 1954 Poliomyelitis Vaccine Trials: Summary Report.” American Journal of Public Health 45 (5): 1–63.


National Cancer Institute. 2024. How Do Clinical Trials Work? National Cancer Institute. https://www.cancer.gov/research/participate/clinical-trials/how-trials-work.


Porter, Jane, and Hershel Jick. 1980. “Addiction Rare in Patients Treated with Narcotics.” New England Journal of Medicine 302 (2): 123. https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJM198001103020221.


U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, Office for Human Research Protections. 2026. Federal Policy for the Protection of Human Subjects (Common Rule). HHS OHRP. https://www.hhs.gov/ohrp/regulations-and-policy/regulations/common-rule/index.html.


World Medical Association. 2024. WMA Declaration of Helsinki – Ethical Principles for Medical Research Involving Human Participants. Adopted by the 75th WMA General Assembly, Helsinki, Finland. https://www.wma.net/policies-post/wma-declaration-of-helsinki/.

Ethical research is one reason patient care can improve at all

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 2.4 Research vs. Clinical Care at a Glance
text:
2.4 Research vs. Clinical Care at a Glance








Question
Clinical care
Clinical research




Main goal
Help this patient now
Produce reliable knowledge


Who should benefit first?
The individual patient
Future patients, and sometimes current participants


Who sets the plan?
Clinician and patient
Protocol, eligibility rules, and study design


What governs it?
Professional standards, licensure, ordinary consent
IRB review, protocol rules, research consent (U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, Office for Human Research Protections 2026)


What counts as success?
Good care for this person
Valid evidence that answers the research question

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
2.5 The Physician-Investigator Tension

A clinician may also be a physician-investigator
That can be efficient and even helpful
It can also blur whether advice is being given as a doctor or as a researcher
Modern research ethics assumes this tension is real, not rare (World Medical Association 2024)
That is one reason independent review matters

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
3.1 Why Randomize?

If doctors chose the groups, healthier and sicker patients could cluster without anyone meaning to (National Cancer Institute 2024)
Randomization assigns people by chance
That helps keep the comparison scientifically fair
But it also means the clinician is no longer hand-picking the treatment for this patient
So randomization is ethically important, not just statistically useful

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 3.2 When Is It OK to Randomize a Sick Person?
text:
3.2 When Is It OK to Randomize a Sick Person?

The honest answer needs a concept: clinical equipoise
It means the expert community is genuinely unsure which arm is better
If we already knew, randomizing would be wrong — we’d be assigning some patients to the worse option on purpose
Equipoise is the moral license for a trial
This is why “I’d just give everyone the new drug” misses the point: we do not yet know it is better

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 3.3 One Map of a Parallel-Group Trial
text:
3.3 One Map of a Parallel-Group Trial







rct


A

People with the
condition



B

Eligibility check
and consent



A->B





C

Randomize



B->C





D

Arm 1
new treatment



C->D





E

Arm 2
standard care or
control



C->E





F

Follow-up
and outcome data



D->F





E->F





G

Compare results



F->G






 A plain-language map of a parallel-group randomized trial. The key ethical moment is the middle: once participants consent and are randomized, no one chooses the arm just for that patient’s benefit.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
3.6 What the Control Group Gets

Sometimes the control group gets current standard care
Sometimes it gets standard care plus a placebo
Sometimes, if no proven treatment exists, it may get placebo or no active treatment (National Cancer Institute 2024; World Medical Association 2024)
Control does not mean “ignored”
Everyone in the study is still monitored under a protocol

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
3.7 Placebo Does Not Always Mean “Nothing”

A placebo can do different jobs.

If no proven treatment exists, placebo may stand in for no active treatment.
If a proven treatment already exists, placebo is often added on top of standard care so no one is denied basic treatment.
The ethical question is not just “placebo or no placebo?” It is what participants are missing by being in that arm (National Cancer Institute 2024; World Medical Association 2024).

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
3.8 What Blinding Actually Does

Blinding means some people in the trial do not know which arm a participant is in
Single-blind usually means the participant does not know
Double-blind usually means the participant and the treating team or evaluators do not know
The point is to reduce expectation effects and biased judgment
“Double-blind” is about cleaner evidence, not secrecy for its own sake

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
3.7 A Tiny Example

If patients know they are receiving the new drug, they may report feeling better partly because they expect it to work. If clinicians know who got the new drug, they may judge borderline improvements more generously. Blinding tries to reduce that problem.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 3.10 Who Watches the Trial While It Runs?
text:
3.10 Who Watches the Trial While It Runs?

A trial is not left alone until it ends
An independent data safety monitoring board (DSMB) reviews the data as it accumulates
It can stop a trial early for harm — one arm is clearly hurting people
It can also stop early for benefit — one arm is so clearly better that continuing the other is no longer fair
Equipoise has a deadline: once the evidence is in, the trial must end

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
5.1 Four Phases, Four Questions








Phase
The main question
Typical scale




I
Is it safe? What dose can people tolerate?
Small


II
Does it seem to work?
Larger


III
Is it better than the current standard?
Large, often randomized


IV
What happens after approval in the real world?
Very large, longer-term

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
5.2 One Simple Phase Map







phases


A

Phase I
small group
safety and dose



B

Phase II
more participants
early evidence of effect



A->B





C

Phase III
large comparison
against standard care



B->C





D

Phase IV
after approval
long-term follow-up



C->D






 A plain-language phase map. The main ethical point is that ‘clinical trial’ covers activities with very different goals, risks, and likely benefits.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
4.3 Phase I: Start with Safety

In cancer research, Phase I often involves about 15 to 30 people (National Cancer Institute 2024)
Researchers ask what dose is tolerable and what harms appear
They also look for signs the intervention is doing anything biologically
But direct benefit is not the main point
This is why Phase I can be ethically difficult: risk may be significant while personal upside is limited

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 4.4 Phase II: Does It Look Promising?
text:
4.4 Phase II: Does It Look Promising?

More people are enrolled than in Phase I
The question shifts toward whether the treatment seems to work
Safety is still being watched closely
A positive Phase II result is encouraging, not conclusive
Many interventions fail here or later

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 4.5 Phase III: Against the Current Standard
text:
4.5 Phase III: Against the Current Standard

Phase III asks whether a new treatment outperforms the best existing option or changes the balance of harms and benefits
Randomization is common here (National Cancer Institute 2024)
Sample sizes are much larger
This is where placebo and control-group debates become most visible
The outcome matters not just for one patient but for what becomes the new standard

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
4.6 Phase IV: After Approval

Approval is not the end of learning
Phase IV tracks long-term safety and effectiveness in broader populations (National Cancer Institute 2024)
Rare side effects may appear only after many people use a drug
Real-world use is messier than tightly controlled trials
So ethics does not stop once a treatment reaches the market

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
5.4 Why Phases Matter Ethically

Students often hear “clinical trial” and imagine one thing
But Phase I and Phase IV do not ask the same question or offer the same likely benefits
Consent should match the phase, not rely on a generic script
Risks, hopes, and expectations shift from phase to phase
So the ethical explanation has to be specific

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
6.1 The Therapeutic Misconception

Therapeutic misconception happens when participants think the study intervention is being chosen mainly for their personal medical benefit (Appelbaum et al. 1982)
The misunderstanding is not just “I hope this helps”
It is more like “my doctor is selecting this because it is best for me”
That is often false once protocol rules and randomization control the options
A person can sign a form and still misunderstand the basic situation

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
6.2 Why It Happens So Easily

Recruitment often comes from a trusted clinician
Serious illness makes hope urgent
Research visits look like treatment visits
Words like “option” and “opportunity” blur categories
Many people hear “new” and assume “better”

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 6.3 A Research Consent Is Not a Treatment Consent
text:
6.3 A Research Consent Is Not a Treatment Consent

Valid consent has four elements; research adds requirements on top of them
It must say, in plain words, “this is research” — not treatment chosen for you
It must disclose randomization: a chance process, not your clinician, may pick your arm
It must state the goal is knowledge, and that direct benefit is uncertain or unlikely
It must make withdrawal at any time, without penalty, explicit
The nurse is often the one who notices a form was signed but not understood

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
6.4 The Dangerous Sentence


“This study may help you” can be true. “This study is being chosen because it is the best treatment for you” is often false.


The distinction is what the therapeutic misconception tries to protect (Appelbaum et al. 1982; Emanuel et al. 2000).

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 6.5 Early-Phase Trials and Thin Personal Benefit
text:
6.5 Early-Phase Trials and Thin Personal Benefit

Healthy volunteers and desperate patients join studies for very different reasons
In Phase I, researchers mainly ask what dose is tolerable and what harms appear
Some participants may benefit, but many will not
Payment, hope, and lack of alternatives can all cloud judgment
Ethical recruitment has to say this plainly

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
4.5 Thought Question

A new drug’s Phase III trial is reported as “positive.”

It significantly improved a surrogate marker on a scan.
The trial was small, and about 25% of participants dropped out.
The analysis included only those who completed.


Name the three weaknesses you can already see.
What would you want to know before telling a patient this drug “works”?
Is “the FDA might approve it” the same as “it helps patients”?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
4.6 Make It Contemporary

Regulators sometimes grant accelerated approval based on a surrogate endpoint, with a confirmatory trial promised later.

Patients with no other option get the drug years sooner.
Some confirmatory trials later show no real benefit — and a few are never finished.

Is faster access worth weaker evidence? Who should decide, and what should happen if the confirmatory trial fails?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
4.7 After You’ve Discussed






Note



Three distinctions to carry forward:

Statistically significant ≠ clinically meaningful. A real but tiny effect on a surrogate can be true and still not worth having.
Regulatory approval ≠ proven patient benefit. Approval is a decision under uncertainty, not a guarantee.
The standing questions are always the same: what was measured, in whom, and who is missing from the result?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
7.1 The Main Distinction

Trials are ethically hard because they borrow the setting of care but not its single-minded goal
Randomization, blinding, control groups, and phases are scientific tools
The same tools also let you read a result: what was measured, could it be chance, who is missing
Those tools are exactly why research needs its own ethics
Part B turns from the structure of trials to the rulebook meant to protect the people inside them

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
7.2 Wrapping Up Part A

Clinical research is not ordinary care with extra paperwork. Its purpose is to produce reliable knowledge, which is why it uses randomization, control groups, blinding, and staged phases — visible in one real instrument, the 1954 polio trial, and again in the COVID-19 vaccine trials. Those same tools also teach you to read a study: clinical versus surrogate endpoints, whether a result could be chance, and who dropped out or was left out. The key task in Part A is learning to say, clearly and without panic: this is research, not just treatment by another name — and here is how good its evidence actually is.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
7.3 References


Appelbaum, Paul S., Loren H. Roth, and Charles Lidz. 1982. “The Therapeutic Misconception: Informed Consent in Psychiatric Research.” International Journal of Law and Psychiatry 5 (3-4): 319–29. https://doi.org/10.1016/0160-2527(82)90026-7.


Emanuel, Ezekiel J., David Wendler, and Christine Grady. 2000. “What Makes Clinical Research Ethical?” JAMA 283 (20): 2701–11. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.283.20.2701.


Francis, Thomas et al. 1955. “An Evaluation of the 1954 Poliomyelitis Vaccine Trials: Summary Report.” American Journal of Public Health 45 (5): 1–63.


National Cancer Institute. 2024. How Do Clinical Trials Work? National Cancer Institute. https://www.cancer.gov/research/participate/clinical-trials/how-trials-work.


Porter, Jane, and Hershel Jick. 1980. “Addiction Rare in Patients Treated with Narcotics.” New England Journal of Medicine 302 (2): 123. https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJM198001103020221.


U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, Office for Human Research Protections. 2026. Federal Policy for the Protection of Human Subjects (Common Rule). HHS OHRP. https://www.hhs.gov/ohrp/regulations-and-policy/regulations/common-rule/index.html.


World Medical Association. 2024. WMA Declaration of Helsinki – Ethical Principles for Medical Research Involving Human Participants. Adopted by the 75th WMA General Assembly, Helsinki, Finland. https://www.wma.net/policies-post/wma-declaration-of-helsinki/.

title: Case 1 — Dax Cowart: ‘Please Let Me Die’
text:
Who: Donald “Dax” Cowart (1947–2019), a 25-year-old Air Force pilot from Texas
What: Severely burned in a propane explosion in 1973; demanded treatment be stopped throughout two years of intensely painful care; was overruled and treated against his wishes
Where / When: Parkland Hospital (Dallas) and the University of Texas Medical Branch (Galveston), 1973–1975
Why it matters: Cowart’s case became the most-discussed example in American bioethics of the conflict between patient autonomy (the right to refuse treatment) and clinical beneficence (the duty to save a life). Even after he recovered and built a successful career, Cowart insisted his rights had been violated.
Pair with: History & the Clinical Relationship

title: Case 1 — Dax Cowart: ‘Please Let Me Die’
text:
The Case
In July 1973, Dax Cowart and his father, Ray, drove to a piece of rural land in east Texas that Ray was thinking of buying. A propane line under the property had ruptured, and the gas had pooled invisibly in a low area near where they parked. When Ray turned the car’s ignition to leave, the spark detonated the gas. Ray died three months later from his injuries. Dax, then 25, survived — but burns covered roughly 65% of his body, including deep (third-degree) burns to his face, hands, and upper body. He was blinded in both eyes, lost most of his fingers, and lost both ears.
He was conscious throughout. According to multiple accounts, the first words he spoke to a passing farmer at the scene were a request to be shot.
He was admitted first to Parkland Memorial Hospital in Dallas, then transferred to the burn unit at the University of Texas Medical Branch in Galveston, where he would spend most of the next 14 months. The standard treatment for burns of this severity at that time — and largely still today — required daily debridement: the removal of dead and infected tissue to allow new skin to grow and to prevent fatal sepsis. At UTMB, debridement was performed in a Hubbard tank, a stainless-steel pool of warm chlorinated water in which the patient’s burned tissue was scrubbed away. Patients commonly described the procedure as the worst pain of their lives.
From very early in his treatment, Cowart asked that the tankings be stopped, that no further skin grafts be performed, and that he be allowed to die. He asked his physicians, his nurses, and his mother. He asked repeatedly, persistently, and — psychiatrists who evaluated him concluded — competently. He understood his prognosis. He understood that refusing treatment would mean a death from infection within weeks. He preferred that to continuing.
His treating physicians — most prominently Dr. Charles Baxter, a pioneering burn specialist — refused. Cowart’s mother, Ada, a devout Baptist, served as his legal surrogate and authorized the continuation of treatment. The team accepted her authorization. For two years, he was forcibly medicated, restrained when necessary, and treated against his stated wishes.
He survived. He returned home blind, severely disabled, and dependent on others for many basic tasks. He attempted suicide twice. He eventually went to law school, earned his J.D. from Texas Tech in 1986, married, divorced, and built a career as a practicing attorney and public speaker.
For the rest of his life, Cowart said two things that puzzled many listeners. First: he was glad to be alive. Second: his physicians had been wrong to keep him alive against his will. The two statements, he insisted, did not contradict each other. The question, for him, was never whether the outcome was good — it was whether the choice had been his to make.
He died in 2019.

title: Case 1 — Dax Cowart: ‘Please Let Me Die’
text:
Context Students Need

Burn injury severity. Burns are classified by depth. First-degree burns affect only the outer skin layer (sunburn). Second-degree burns reach into deeper layers and blister. Third-degree (full-thickness) burns destroy all skin layers and the nerve endings underneath. Counterintuitively, the deepest burns are often not the most painful — but the surrounding partial-thickness burns are. Cowart’s burns covered roughly 65% of his “total body surface area” (TBSA), with much of it third-degree. In 1973, mortality at that severity exceeded 50%; survival required months of grafting and debridement.


Debridement and the Hubbard tank. New skin will not grow over dead tissue, and dead tissue invites infection that can kill within days. Debridement — physically removing that tissue — is essential. The Hubbard tank used at UTMB allowed warm water and saline to soften the eschar (dead skin) so that nurses could scrub it off. The procedure is shorter and less brutal today, with better anesthesia and surgical alternatives, but the underlying necessity remains.


Informed consent in 1973. The legal doctrine of informed consent was very new. Canterbury v. Spence, the federal case that established the modern “reasonable patient” standard for disclosure, had been decided just one year earlier (1972). Most physicians of Dr. Baxter’s generation had been trained in a model where the doctor decided what was best and the patient cooperated. Patient refusal of life-saving treatment was treated, by default, as evidence that the patient was not thinking clearly. (For the long arc by which informed consent emerged as a doctrine, see Faden and Beauchamp (1986).)


Decision-making capacity. “Capacity” is a clinical term distinct from “competence” (a legal term). A patient has capacity to make a specific decision if they can (1) understand the relevant information, (2) appreciate how it applies to their situation, (3) reason about treatment options, and (4) communicate a choice. Capacity is decision-specific and time-specific — a patient may have capacity to refuse one treatment but not another, or capacity today but not tomorrow. Multiple psychiatrists evaluated Cowart and found that he had capacity.


Surrogate decision-making. When a patient lacks capacity, a surrogate (often a family member) decides on their behalf. The American standard hierarchy is substituted judgment (decide what the patient would have wanted) followed by best interest (decide what serves the patient’s welfare). Critically: surrogates have no authority to override a patient who does have capacity. In Cowart’s case, his mother’s religious convictions were given controlling weight even though Cowart himself was found capable of deciding.

title: Case 1 — Dax Cowart: ‘Please Let Me Die’
text:
Ethical Issues
The case is usually framed as a collision between two of the four principles of biomedical ethics:
Autonomy. Cowart was a competent adult. He understood his condition and his prognosis. He chose, repeatedly and consistently, to refuse treatment. The standard view in modern bioethics is that competent patients have an essentially unconditional right to refuse any medical intervention, including life-saving care. On this view, his treatment team violated his rights, full stop.
Beneficence and non-maleficence. Cowart’s physicians believed — correctly, as it turned out — that he could survive and eventually rebuild a meaningful life. They saw their refusal to stop treatment as an act of mercy and skill, not coercion. From their perspective, allowing a patient in extremis to make an irreversible decision that he might later regret was a failure of medical responsibility, not a respect for it.
Justice and the role of family. The team’s deference to Cowart’s mother raises a different question: under what circumstances should family preferences override a competent patient’s expressed wishes? The case shows how easily institutional power can route around a patient’s voice when family and medical authority align.
A subtler issue runs underneath all of this: the problem of retrospective endorsement. Cowart’s later statement that he was glad to be alive is sometimes invoked as a justification for what was done to him. (“See? He thanks us now.”) Cowart himself rejected this argument. The fact that an outcome turned out well does not, he argued, retroactively legitimize a decision that was not the patient’s to make. A surgeon who operates on the wrong leg cannot defend the act by pointing out that the patient walked again.

title: Case 1 — Dax Cowart: ‘Please Let Me Die’
text:
Multiple Perspectives
Dax Cowart himself insisted throughout his life that the question was about control over his own body, not about outcome. “I do not want to die because the quality of life is too low,” he told an interviewer in 1989. “I want to die because the quality of life is too low and I am the one who has to live it.” His position was libertarian about autonomy in the strong sense: a competent person’s right to refuse extends even to refusals others find tragic or mistaken.
The treating physicians, particularly Dr. Charles Baxter, defended their decisions in interviews and in the films made about the case. Baxter argued that severely burned patients in acute pain frequently ask to die early in treatment and almost universally change their minds once they have recovered. To honor the early refusal, on this view, would be to mistake an episode of suffering for a settled preference.
Ada Cowart, Dax’s mother, was a religious woman who believed life was a gift from God that her son had no right to refuse. She authorized continued treatment as both a parent and a Christian. She lived to see her son recover; her authorization is, in her own framework, vindicated.
Philosophers and legal scholars have used the case to develop competing accounts. Robert Burt, in a well-known dialogue with Cowart (Cowart and Burt 1998), argued that the relationship between physician and suffering patient cannot be modeled as a simple consent transaction — that the demand to die was, in part, a demand to be heard, and that the right response was not always to comply with the literal request. Cowart pushed back hard.
Disability rights advocates have noted that Cowart’s case prefigures contemporary debates about medical assistance in dying. They observe a recurring asymmetry: when an able-bodied person refuses care, clinicians often comply; when a newly disabled person refuses care, clinicians often resist. The implicit assumption — that life with disability is intolerable, but that this assumption can be overridden by treatment — is, they argue, exactly backwards.

title: Case 1 — Dax Cowart: ‘Please Let Me Die’
text:
Discussion Questions

Was Dax Cowart’s refusal of treatment a refusal that his physicians should have honored? Defend your answer using at least one of the four principles.
Cowart later said he was glad to be alive and that his rights had been violated. Are these two claims consistent? What does your answer imply about how we should evaluate paternalistic decisions in retrospect?
Cowart’s mother authorized continued treatment as his legal surrogate. Under modern standards of decision-making capacity, was her authorization valid? Should it have been given any weight at all if Cowart was competent?
Dr. Baxter argued that severely burned patients almost always change their minds about wanting to die after they recover. Suppose this is statistically true. Does it justify overriding any individual patient’s refusal? Why or why not?
Compare Cowart’s case to a contemporary patient who requests medical aid in dying (MAID) for a terminal illness. What features of the cases are similar, and what features are different?
Imagine you are a nurse in the burn unit assigned to perform the daily tankings. Cowart has just told you, again, that he wants to stop and be allowed to die. What do you do, and what do you say? What do you owe him in this moment that the institution does not owe him?
Some disability rights scholars argue that clinicians systematically underestimate the future quality of life of newly disabled patients. If true, how should that empirical fact change the ethical analysis of Cowart’s case?

title: Case 1 — Dax Cowart: ‘Please Let Me Die’
text:
Further Reading

Please Let Me Die (1974) — documentary film featuring Cowart during his treatment.
Dax’s Case (1985) — follow-up documentary, widely used in bioethics courses.
Lonnie D. Kliever, ed., Dax’s Case: Essays in Medical Ethics and Human Meaning (Southern Methodist University Press, 1989).
Keith Burton, “A Chronicle: Dax’s Case as It Happened,” Hastings Center Report 19, no. 1 (1989): 5–10.
Obituary: “Donald ‘Dax’ Cowart, Subject of Landmark Bioethics Case, Dies at 71,” Texas Monthly, 2019.

title: Case 1 — Dax Cowart: ‘Please Let Me Die’
text:
References


Cowart, Dax, and Robert Burt. 1998. “Confronting Death: Who Chooses, Who Controls?” Hastings Center Report 28 (1): 14–24. https://doi.org/10.2307/3528337.


Faden, Ruth R., and Tom L. Beauchamp. 1986. A History and Theory of Informed Consent. Oxford University Press.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
By the end of Part A, you will be able to:

Define bioethics and explain why it emerged as a distinct field
Place bioethics on the map of ethics: descriptive, metaethical, and normative (theoretical vs. practical)
Distinguish ethical claims from legal, religious, and personal-preference claims

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
You will also be able to:

Explain the four principles as an explication of common morality
Balance and specify principles — and say when one may override another
Diagnose the source of a moral conflict, and tell a moral conflict from a moral dilemma
Identify premises, conclusions, hidden assumptions, and common fallacies

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.1 Where Bioethics Sits
“Ethics” names three different activities, not one:








Branch
The question it asks
How it works




Descriptive ethics
What do people actually believe and do?
Surveys, history, sociology, psychology


Metaethics
What are moral claims — true? objective? just feelings?
Conceptual and logical analysis


Normative ethics
What ought we to do, and why?
Reasoned argument, principles, theory



Bioethics draws on all three — but its central job is normative.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.2 Descriptive and Metaethical Questions
Descriptive ethics — the empirical study of moral belief and behavior. It reports; it does not prescribe.

Example: “In one survey, most ICU nurses reported moral distress about treatment they considered futile.” A fact about attitudes — not yet a claim about what is right.

Metaethics — steps back to ask about morality itself.

Example: “When we say ‘this is wrong,’ are we stating a fact, expressing a feeling, or applying a shared standard?”







Note



Bioethics uses descriptive facts (you cannot weigh a harm you have not measured) and usually brackets metaethics — clinicians must act before philosophy settles whether morality is “objective.”

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.3 Normative Ethics: Theory and Practice
Normative ethics asks which actions, policies, and characters are right — and what reasons support that. It works at two levels:








Level
What it does
Examples




Theoretical
Builds general accounts of rightness
Utilitarianism, Kantian ethics (Part B)


Practical / applied
Brings moral norms into a real domain
Bioethics, business ethics, environmental ethics




Bioethics is applied normative ethics: real cases, real decisions
It still leans on descriptive facts beneath it and moral theory behind it
Most of this course lives here: what should we do for this patient, this study, this policy — and why?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: 5.2 What the Principles Are Made Of
text:
5.2 What the Principles Are Made Of
Beauchamp and Childress did not invent a new morality. They drew the four principles out of the common morality.

Common morality — the basic moral norms that essentially all morally serious people already accept, across cultures and eras
It is pre-theoretical: you do not need a philosophy course to know it
It is shared ground: a Kantian, a utilitarian, and a religious believer mostly agree on it

I call it “fourth-grade morality” — the rules a thoughtful ten-year-old already follows.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
5.3 “Fourth-Grade Morality,” Made Precise
Norms almost no one seriously rejects — and what each becomes in medicine:



Rule a child already knows
In the clinic it becomes…




“Ask first; don’t push people around”
Autonomy


“Help people who are hurt”
Beneficence


“Don’t hurt people”
Non-maleficence


“Share fairly; no favorites”
Justice


“Don’t lie; keep your promises”
Truth-telling, consent, fidelity




The principles are an explication of this shared morality
Explication = take a rough concept we already use and make it precise and usable — without replacing it

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
5.4 Why “Common Morality” Matters

Authority — the principles are not two philosophers’ opinions; they restate what we already believe
Reach — people who disagree about religion, politics, or theory can still share them
Limits — common morality is general: “do no harm” does not tell you whether this risky surgery is a harm worth taking







Note



This is why the four principles are a starting point, not an answer key. The real work — coming next — is turning shared general norms into a decision in one specific case.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: 5.14 Principles Are Prima Facie, Not Absolute
text:
5.14 Principles Are Prima Facie, Not Absolute
Prima facie (“at first sight”) — a principle is binding unless, in this case, it is outweighed by a stronger competing principle. None of the four is absolute.
Balancing — the deliberate weighing of competing principles in the situation in front of you.

There is no fixed ranking: autonomy does not always beat beneficence, or vice versa
Balancing is judgment — but not whim: you must give reasons another reasonable person could weigh
Example: a competent patient refuses a feeding tube. Autonomy and non-maleficence pull one way, beneficence the other. Balancing usually favors the refusal — if the reasons are stated, not just felt

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: 5.15 When May One Principle Override Another?
text:
5.15 When May One Principle Override Another?
Disciplined balancing. Before letting principle X override principle Y, you should be able to say:

Effective — overriding Y will realistically achieve the aim
Necessary — no weaker option would work
Least infringement — you intrude on Y as little as possible
Proportionate — the good gained outweighs the norm set aside
Consistent — you would judge any relevantly similar case the same way

Example: warning one named, seriously threatened person can meet these conditions; announcing a patient’s diagnosis “just to be safe” cannot.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: 5.16 Specification: From General Norm to Working Rule
text:
5.16 Specification: From General Norm to Working Rule
Specification — narrowing a broad principle into concrete, action-guiding rules for particular contexts.







Specified through…
“Respect autonomy” becomes…




Law
The right to refuse treatment; informed-consent statutes


Institutional policy
The hospital’s consent form and capacity workflow


Professional codes
Nursing confidentiality and scope-of-practice duties


Clinical protocol
When a second clinician must confirm capacity




Balancing decides this case; specification builds standing rules so we do not re-argue every case from zero

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: 5.17 Why Do Careful People Still Disagree?
text:
5.17 Why Do Careful People Still Disagree?
Most — though not all — people accept the common morality. So deep disagreement in bioethics is usually not good vs. evil. It has four main sources:








Source
What is really in dispute
Example




Facts
The empirical situation
“How likely is this harm?”


Balancing
How to weigh shared norms here
Autonomy vs. beneficence in a refusal


Specification
Which concrete rule the norm becomes
What counts as informed consent


Deep value clash
A real disagreement at the margins
The moral status of an embryo



Naming the source is the first move: a factual dispute is resolved very differently from a values dispute.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
5.18 Moral Conflict vs. Moral Dilemma

Moral conflict — moral reasons pull both ways, but one course is, on balance, better justified. Most hard cases are like this.
Moral dilemma — there are strong moral reasons for incompatible actions and no clean option: the better choice still breaks a real obligation.
Moral residue — the regret or duty-to-repair that remains even when you choose well. Its presence signals a true dilemma, not just a tough call.


Example. Two patients, one ICU bed, equal need. Choosing is not “solving” it — the patient not chosen was still owed care. The residue you feel is appropriate, not irrational.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Today — Foundations

By the end of Part A, you will be able to:

Define bioethics and explain why it emerged as a distinct field
Place bioethics on the map of ethics: descriptive, metaethical, and normative (theoretical vs. practical)
Distinguish ethical claims from legal, religious, and personal-preference claims

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Today — The Toolkit

You will also be able to:

Explain the four principles as an explication of common morality
Balance and specify principles — and say when one may override another
Diagnose the source of a moral conflict, and tell a moral conflict from a moral dilemma
Identify premises, conclusions, hidden assumptions, and common fallacies

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Where Bioethics Sits
“Ethics” names three different activities, not one:








Branch
The question it asks
How it works




Descriptive ethics
What do people actually believe and do?
Surveys, history, sociology, psychology


Metaethics
What are moral claims — true? objective? just feelings?
Conceptual and logical analysis


Normative ethics
What ought we to do, and why?
Reasoned argument, principles, theory



Bioethics draws on all three — but its central job is normative.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Descriptive and Metaethical Questions
Descriptive ethics — the empirical study of moral belief and behavior. It reports; it does not prescribe.

Example: “In one survey, most ICU nurses reported moral distress about treatment they considered futile.” A fact about attitudes — not yet a claim about what is right.

Metaethics — steps back to ask about morality itself.

Example: “When we say ‘this is wrong,’ are we stating a fact, expressing a feeling, or applying a shared standard?”







Note


Bioethics uses descriptive facts (you cannot weigh a harm you have not measured) and usually brackets metaethics — clinicians must act before philosophy settles whether morality is “objective.”

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Normative Ethics: Theory and Practice
Normative ethics asks which actions, policies, and characters are right — and what reasons support that. It works at two levels:








Level
What it does
Examples




Theoretical
Builds general accounts of rightness
Utilitarianism, Kantian ethics (Part B)


Practical / applied
Brings moral norms into a real domain
Bioethics, business ethics, environmental ethics




Bioethics is applied normative ethics: real cases, real decisions
It still leans on descriptive facts beneath it and moral theory behind it
Most of this course lives here: what should we do for this patient, this study, this policy — and why?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: What the Principles Are Made Of
text:
What the Principles Are Made Of
Beauchamp and Childress did not invent a new morality. They drew the four principles out of the common morality.

Common morality — the basic moral norms that essentially all morally serious people already accept, across cultures and eras
It is pre-theoretical: you do not need a philosophy course to know it
It is shared ground: a Kantian, a utilitarian, and a religious believer mostly agree on it

I call it “fourth-grade morality” — the rules a thoughtful ten-year-old already follows.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
“Fourth-Grade Morality,” Made Precise
Norms almost no one seriously rejects — and what each becomes in medicine:



Rule a child already knows
In the clinic it becomes…




“Ask first; don’t push people around”
Autonomy


“Help people who are hurt”
Beneficence


“Don’t hurt people”
Non-maleficence


“Share fairly; no favorites”
Justice


“Don’t lie; keep your promises”
Truth-telling, consent, fidelity




The principles are an explication of this shared morality
Explication = take a rough concept we already use and make it precise and usable — without replacing it

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Why “Common Morality” Matters

Authority — the principles are not two philosophers’ opinions; they restate what we already believe
Reach — people who disagree about religion, politics, or theory can still share them
Limits — common morality is general: “do no harm” does not tell you whether this risky surgery is a harm worth taking







Note


This is why the four principles are a starting point, not an answer key. The real work — coming next — is turning shared general norms into a decision in one specific case.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: Principles Are Prima Facie, Not Absolute
text:
Principles Are Prima Facie, Not Absolute
Prima facie (“at first sight”) — a principle is binding unless, in this case, it is outweighed by a stronger competing principle. None of the four is absolute.
Balancing — the deliberate weighing of competing principles in the situation in front of you.

There is no fixed ranking: autonomy does not always beat beneficence, or vice versa
Balancing is judgment — but not whim: you must give reasons another reasonable person could weigh
Example: a competent patient refuses a feeding tube. Autonomy and non-maleficence pull one way, beneficence the other. Balancing usually favors the refusal — if the reasons are stated, not just felt

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: When May One Principle Override Another?
text:
When May One Principle Override Another?
Disciplined balancing. Before letting principle X override principle Y, you should be able to say:

Effective — overriding Y will realistically achieve the aim
Necessary — no weaker option would work
Least infringement — you intrude on Y as little as possible
Proportionate — the good gained outweighs the norm set aside
Consistent — you would judge any relevantly similar case the same way

Example: warning one named, seriously threatened person can meet these conditions; announcing a patient’s diagnosis “just to be safe” cannot.

text:

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.1 Utilitarianism, Followed All the Way

Peter Singer is the most influential living utilitarian, known for following the theory’s logic wherever it leads (Singer 2011).
He defends preference utilitarianism: an act is right if it best satisfies the preferences of everyone affected, counted impartially.
His master rule is the principle of equal consideration of interests: like interests get equal weight no matter whose they are.
Impartiality is literal — your interests get no extra weight for being yours, your family’s, your country’s, or your species’.
This makes the theory powerful and uncomfortable: it generates strong, sometimes radical, real-world obligations.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.2 Singer on Global Obligations

The drowning child. You pass a shallow pond where a small child is drowning. Wading in ruins your expensive shoes — obviously you must save her. Singer asks: why is a child dying of a preventable disease overseas any different? (Singer 1972)


His principle: if we can prevent something very bad without sacrificing anything of comparable importance, we must — and distance does not change that.
It launched the effective altruism movement: give substantially, where each dollar does the most good.
The standard pushback is the demandingness objection — strictly applied, it seems to require giving until you are nearly as badly off as those you help.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.3 Singer on Animals

In Animal Liberation (1975), Singer extended equal consideration beyond our own species (Singer 1975).
The morally relevant feature is sentience — the capacity to suffer — not intelligence, language, or species.
Giving humans’ interests automatic priority over animals’ like interests is speciesism, which he argues is, in form, like racism or sexism.
This is not the claim that animals and humans are equal in all respects — only that comparable suffering deserves comparable weight.
On these grounds he judges factory farming and much animal research to be vast, unjustified harm.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: 3.4 Singer at the Edges of Life
text:
3.4 Singer at the Edges of Life

Singer separates being biologically human from being a person — a self-aware being with preferences about its own future.
The wrongness of killing then tracks personhood and preferences, not species membership by itself.
So he holds abortion permissible (an early fetus is not yet a person) and defends voluntary euthanasia.
Most controversially, he argues that for some severely impaired newborns, ending life may be permissible.
The disability-rights objection is sharp — this rates disabled lives as lower-value, a challenge we return to later.


Why teach the hardest case: Singer matters precisely because he refuses to stop where intuition gets uncomfortable. This slide is the hinge — the next section asks whether “follow the calculation wherever it leads” is the right method at all.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
5.1 Answering Singer

If Singer is utilitarianism with the gloves off, the Kantian tradition is the main reply.
The separateness of persons: people are not interchangeable vessels of welfare, so a serious harm to one is not cancelled by benefits to others.
Morality includes constraints: using, deceiving, or killing an innocent person can be wrong even when it would maximize good.
Doing vs. allowing: actively harming someone differs from failing to benefit a stranger — so the “distance and numbers don’t matter” move is contested.
The reply is not “ignore consequences”; it is “consequences are not the only moral currency.”

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: 5.2 Onora O’Neill: Principled Autonomy and Trust
text:
5.2 Onora O’Neill: Principled Autonomy and Trust

O’Neill is a leading contemporary Kantian working directly on bioethics (O’Neill 2002).
She argues bioethics has shrunk autonomy to “the patient chose it” — mere preference-satisfaction, close to Singer’s currency.
Real Kantian autonomy is principled: acting on principles one could will everyone to follow, not just doing what one wants.
She refocuses the field on trust and trustworthiness — what clinicians and institutions owe, not only what patients may choose.
So a consent form no reasonable person could actually trust is not genuine respect for autonomy.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: 5.3 Frances Kamm: Why the Numbers Don’t Just Add Up
text:
5.3 Frances Kamm: Why the Numbers Don’t Just Add Up

Kamm is the most rigorous living nonconsequentialist, dissecting exactly when harms and numbers may be traded (Kamm 2013).
She defends constraints: some things you may not do to a person even to produce more total good.
Via the trolley problem and the Doctrine of Double Effect, how a harm comes about matters — intended versus foreseen.
She recognizes prerogatives: you need not sacrifice everything to maximize good — a direct hit at Singer’s demandingness.
This licenses what utilitarianism struggles with: refusing to harvest one patient to save five, though five outnumber one.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: 5.4 Singer vs. the Kantians, Side by Side
text:
5.4 Singer vs. the Kantians, Side by Side








Issue
Singer (preference utilitarian)
Kantian reply (O’Neill, Kamm)




Global poverty
Strong duty to give; distance irrelevant; very demanding
Duties of justice and trust to particular others; aid matters but is bounded


Animals
Equal consideration of like interests; speciesism is a bias
Treatment is still constrained, but duties often track rational agency


End of life
Personhood and preferences decide; voluntary euthanasia permissible
Persons have dignity; you may not use or kill a person for net benefit


Method
Sum the interests, choose the maximum
Apply constraints first; numbers do not settle everything



Same cases, different moral currency — which is exactly why the course keeps both lenses on the table.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
6.1 One Family, Four Traditions
These traditions judge the agent and the relationship, not only the isolated act — but they differ sharply:

Aristotelian virtue ethics — flourishing through stable, cultivated traits of character.
Care ethics — morality begins in concrete, dependent relationships; even Beauchamp and Childress list care among the focal virtues (Beauchamp and Childress 2019).
Confucian ethics — the self is constituted by roles and relationships, refined through lifelong practice (Fan 1999).
Natural law — reason discerns the basic human goods every life needs, and the duty to pursue them (Gómez-Lobo 2002).

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
6.6 Care as a Virtue

Care ethics treats responsiveness to others as a cultivated disposition, not a rule to apply.
It grew from Carol Gilligan’s claim that mainstream theory missed a whole moral “voice” (Gilligan 1982).
Virginia Held built it into a full account in which relationship and dependency are morally basic (Held 2006).
The good clinician’s care is itself a trait — attentiveness, responsiveness, responsibility — like an Aristotelian virtue.
Its risk mirrors its strength: relationships can be unequal or controlling, so care still needs justice.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
6.9 Confucian Virtue Ethics



One of the world’s oldest virtue traditions, increasingly important as care globalizes (Fan 1999).
Ren (benevolence, humaneness) is the central virtue: wholehearted concern for others.
Li (ritual propriety) governs how that concern is expressed appropriately within each relationship.
Xiao (filial piety) makes obligations to parents and family morally fundamental, not optional sentiment.
The self is relational and role-constituted — always someone’s child, parent, or clinician — and the exemplar (junzi) is formed over a lifetime of practice.





Confucius

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
6.10 Confucian Ethics in the Clinic

The Confucian frame changes what “respecting the patient” looks like in practice.
Decisions are often family-determined: the family, not the solitary patient, is the natural decision unit.
This collides with Western informed consent — e.g., a family asking that a grave diagnosis not be told directly to an elder.
It is not simply paternalism: it expresses xiao and a duty to shield a loved one from a crushing burden.
So it reframes autonomy as relational rather than individual — a theme that returns when we discuss cross-cultural truth-telling.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
6.11 Natural Law

Rooted in Aristotle and developed by the Catholic theologian Aquinas: reason can read how we ought to live from the goods proper to human nature (Gómez-Lobo 2002).
Its first principle is to do good and avoid evil: pursue, and never act directly against, the basic human goods.
Those basic goods include life and health, knowledge, friendship, and reasonable practical judgment.
Right action respects every basic good — you may not destroy one good simply to promote another.
It is secular in form (argued from reason and human nature) yet underlies the sanctity-of-life language especially common in Catholic bioethics.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
6.12 Natural Law in Bioethics

Natural law gives bioethics one of its most-used tools: the Doctrine of Double Effect (DDE).
DDE permits an act with a grave bad side effect if the harm is foreseen but not intended and the good is proportionate.
Classic case: opioid doses that relieve pain (intended) while possibly hastening death (foreseen) — permissible.
The same logic shapes Catholic health systems on end-of-life and reproductive care.
It draws a line utilitarianism rejects and Kant/Kamm rebuild differently — intending harm vs. allowing it; applied later to palliative sedation in end-of-life care.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
6.13 Four Strands of Character Ethics









Strand
Central idea
Key figure(s)
Bioethics payoff




Aristotelian
Flourishing through virtuous character
Aristotle
The virtues of the good clinician


Care
Morality begins in relationship
Gilligan, Held
The bedside relationship is not a checklist


Confucian
The self is role- and family-constituted
Confucius; Fan
Family-centered decisions; relational autonomy


Natural law
Reason discerns basic human goods
Aquinas
Double effect; sanctity-of-life arguments

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
5.2 Kantian Ethics and “Just Sign Here”

A utilitarian can accept a rushed or spun consent if the outcome is good; a Kantian cannot.
Treating a patient as an end means the consent process must be one a reasonable person could actually trust (O’Neill 2002).
That rules out manipulation, euphemism, and “technically true” disclosure — even when they would steer the patient toward the better outcome.
It shifts the question from “did they sign?” to “did we give them what they would need to decide for themselves?”
Practical upshot: honest disclosure and trustworthy institutions are obligations, not customer service.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: 5.3 Why You Can’t Harvest One to Save Five
text:
5.3 Why You Can’t Harvest One to Save Five

Five patients will die without organs; one healthy person in the waiting room is a match — the arithmetic says harvest.
Kantian ethics says no, flatly: a person is not a resource, even when the numbers are overwhelming (Kamm 2013).
How a harm comes about matters — intending a death differs from foreseeing it, which is why pain relief that may shorten life can still be permissible.
You also have prerogatives: you need not sacrifice everything to maximize good, so Singer’s unlimited demand is rejected.
The bedside payoff: some lines — deceiving, using, or killing a patient — hold even when crossing them would help more people.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: 5.4 Singer vs. Kantian Ethics, Side by Side
text:
5.4 Singer vs. Kantian Ethics, Side by Side








Issue
Singer (preference utilitarian)
Kantian ethics (constraints, dignity)




Global poverty
Strong duty to give; distance irrelevant; very demanding
A real but bounded duty to aid; owed first to particular others


Animals
Equal consideration of like interests; speciesism is a bias
Treatment is still constrained, but duties often track rational agency


End of life
Personhood and preferences decide; voluntary euthanasia permissible
Persons have dignity; you may not use or kill a person for net benefit


Method
Sum the interests, choose the maximum
Apply constraints first; numbers do not settle everything



Same cases, different moral currency — which is why we keep both lenses on the table.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
7.3 Wrapping Up

Bioethics uses a four-principle framework as its shared vocabulary, supported by major ethical theories that answer how to weigh those principles differently. Utilitarianism focuses on outcomes and aggregate welfare — Singer pushes that logic to its hardest conclusions. Kantian ethics focuses on duties and the dignity of persons — O’Neill and Kamm answer Singer by rebuilding moral constraints. The character traditions — virtue, care, Confucian, and natural law — focus on the kind of person, relationship, and community we should sustain. No single theory is correct; used as lenses, they let us see more of what is morally at stake in any clinical situation.
Next, we turn to the history that gave these principles their urgency, and the clinical relationship where they are daily tested.
 All uncredited slide illustrations and scenario visualizations were generated using Gemini.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Kantian Ethics and “Just Sign Here”

A utilitarian can accept a rushed or spun consent if the outcome is good; a Kantian cannot.
Treating a patient as an end means the consent process must be one a reasonable person could actually trust (O’Neill 2002).
That rules out manipulation, euphemism, and “technically true” disclosure — even when they would steer the patient toward the better outcome.
It shifts the question from “did they sign?” to “did we give them what they would need to decide for themselves?”
Practical upshot: honest disclosure and trustworthy institutions are obligations, not customer service.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: Why You Can’t Harvest One to Save Five
text:
Why You Can’t Harvest One to Save Five

Five patients will die without organs; one healthy person in the waiting room is a match — the arithmetic says harvest.
Kantian ethics says no, flatly: a person is not a resource, even when the numbers are overwhelming (Kamm 2013).
How a harm comes about matters — intending a death differs from foreseeing it, which is why pain relief that may shorten life can still be permissible.
You also have prerogatives: you need not sacrifice everything to maximize good, so Singer’s unlimited demand is rejected.
The bedside payoff: some lines — deceiving, using, or killing a patient — hold even when crossing them would help more people.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: Singer vs. Kantian Ethics, Side by Side
text:
Singer vs. Kantian Ethics, Side by Side








Issue
Singer (preference utilitarian)
Kantian ethics (constraints, dignity)




Global poverty
Strong duty to give; distance irrelevant; very demanding
A real but bounded duty to aid; owed first to particular others


Animals
Equal consideration of like interests; speciesism is a bias
Treatment is still constrained, but duties often track rational agency


End of life
Personhood and preferences decide; voluntary euthanasia permissible
Persons have dignity; you may not use or kill a person for net benefit


Method
Sum the interests, choose the maximum
Apply constraints first; numbers do not settle everything



Same cases, different moral currency — which is why we keep both lenses on the table.
